content.name: Sun
description: The Sun is the star at the center of the Solar System, around which all the planets orbit. It is classified as a G-type main-sequence star (G2V).
The visible surface of the Sun is known as the photosphere, where the temperature is around 5,500 °C. The Sun's core reaches a much higher temperature of about 15.7 million °C.

The Sun is primarily composed of hydrogen (about 74%) and helium (about 24%), with trace amounts of heavier elements like oxygen, carbon, neon, and iron. It is a massive, hot ball of plasma, inflated and heated by energy produced by nuclear fusion reactions at its core. Every second, the Sun's core fuses about 600 million tons of hydrogen into helium and converts 4 million tons of matter into energy.

The Sun formed approximately 4.6 billion years ago from the gravitational collapse of matter within a region of a large molecular cloud. Its is now about halfway through its main-sequence stage, and will run out of hydrogen in the core in approximately 5 billion years.

The Sun goes through an 11-year solar cycle, during which the number of sunspots (regions of reduced temperature caused by concentrations of magnetic flux that appear darker on the Sun's surface) and solar flares (localized outbursts of electromagnetic radiation) varies.

content.name: Mercury
description: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun and the smallest in the Solar System. It is one of the four terrestrial planets. Mercury has no natural satellites.

The surface of Mercury is heavily cratered, as a result of multiple impact events that have accumulated over billions of years in the absence of an atmosphere. Its largest crater, Caloris Planitia, has a diameter of 1,550 km and is one-third the diameter of the planet.

Mercury has widely varying surface temperatures, with the equatorial regions ranging from -170 °C at night to 420 °C during sunlight. Due to the very small axial tilt, the planet's poles are permanently shadowed.

Mercury is visible to the naked eye and has been observed since the ancient times. The first spacecraft to visit Mercury was NASA's Mariner 10 in 1974-1975, which mapped about 45% of the planet's surface. The second NASA probe, named MESSENGER, orbited Mercury between 2011 and 2015, collecting a variety of scientific data.

content.name: Venus
description: Venus is the second planet from the Sun. It is a terrestrial planet and is the closest in mass and size to its orbital neighbour Earth. Venus has no natural satellites.

Venus has the densest atmosphere of the terrestrial planets, consisting mostly of carbon dioxide with a thick, global sulfuric acid cloud cover. It has an average surface temperature of 464 °C and an atmospheric pressure 92 times greater than on Earth at sea level.

Venus lacks plate tectonics, which has led to fewer geological features like mountains and valleys compared to Earth. Radar mapping has revealed vast volcanic plains, highland regions, and thousands of volcanoes, some of which may still be active.
Venus is visible to the naked eye, and it is the third brightest object in the Earth's sky. It always appears close to the Sun, as either a "morning star" or an "evening star".

The first spacecraft to fly past Venus was Venera 1 in 1961. In 1966, Venera 3 became the first probe to reach the planet's surface. The first images from the surface were obtained by Venera 9 in 1975. Between 1990 and 1994, Magellan spacecraft operated in orbit and mapped the planet. There have been a few dozen of successful flyby missons.

content.name: Earth
description: Earth is the third planet from the Sun. It is the only known planet to support life. Earth is orbited by one permanent natural satellite, the Moon.

Earth has a stronger magnetic field than other terresrial planets. It prevents the Earth's atmosphere from getting blown away by the solar wind and protects the planet's surface from harmful cosmic radiation.

Earth's atmosphere is primarily composed of nitrogen (78%) and oxygen (21%), with trace amounts of carbon dioxide and other gases. Water vapor is widely present in the atmosphere, forming clouds that cover most of the planet.

Earth is the only planet in the Solar System sustaining liquid surface water. Most of the planet's surface is covered by oceans (70.8%), with the remaining 29.2% is occupied by continents and islands. Earth's surface has various geological features shaped by erosion, tectonic activity, and volcanic eruptions.
Earth has a wide variety of climate zones and ecosystems (forests, grasslands, deserts, tundra, marine and freshwater biomes, polar ice caps), each with its own biodiversity.

moons.name: Moon
description: The Moon is the Earth's only natural satellite.

The Moon's mass is 1.2% of Earth's, and its diameter is roughly one-quarter of Earth's. Within the Solar System, it is the largest and most massive satellite in relation to its parent planet. It is also larger and more massive than all known dwarf planets.

The Moon is tidally locked, which means that the same side of the Moon always faces the Earth. The Moon's surface is covered in lunar dust and is marked by mountains, impact craters, and dark plains of cooled magma.

The first man-made object to reach the Moon was the Luna 2 probe in 1959. In 1969, humans landed on the Moon for the first time, which was also the first human landing on an extraterrestrial body. Five more crews visited the Moon between 1969 and 1972.

content.name: Mars
description: Mars is the fourth planet from the Sun and the outermost terrestrial planet. It is the second smallest planet of the Solar System. Mars has two natural satellites, Phobos and Deimos.

Mars has a thin atmosphere composed mostly of carbon dioxide, with a surface pressure of 6 to 7 millibars, less than 1% of Earth's. The atmosphere is quite dusty due to iron oxide particles suspended in it. Surface temperatures vary from about -110 °C to 35 °C and follow seasonal patterns.

The surface of Mars is orange-red, covered in iron oxide dust. The relatively flat plains in northern parts of Mars strongly contrast with the cratered terrain in southern highlands. Mars hosts many enormous extinct volcanos (such as Olympus Mons, 21.9 km tall) and one of the largest canyons in the Solar System (Valles Marineris, 4,000 km long). The planet's polar ice caps are composed of water and carbon dioxide.

Mars is visible to the naked eye and has been observed since the ancient times. The first flyby was achieved by in 1965 by the Mariner 4 probe. The first spacecraft to orbit the planet was the Mars 2 probe in 1971. The first landing was made by the Viking 1 probe in 1976. Since the early 21st century, Mars has been actively explored by unmanned spacecraft and rovers.

moons.name: Phobos
description: Phobos is the innermost and larger of the two natural satellites of Mars. It is a small, irregularly shaped object.

Phobos orbits closer to Mars than any other known natural satellite of a planet. It orbits Mars much faster than Mars rotates. As a result, from the surface of Mars it appears to rise in the west, move across the sky in 4 hours and 15 minutes or less, and set in the east, twice each Martian day.

Phobos is one of the least reflective bodies in the Solar System. A notable feature of Phobos is the large impact crater, Stickney, which takes up a substantial portion of its surface. Temperatures on Phobos range from about -4 °C on the sunlit side to -112 °C on the shadowed side.

moons.name: Deimos
description: Deimos is the smaller and outer of the two natural satellites of Mars. It is highly non-spherical with the dimensions of 16.1×11.8×10.2 km.

Deimos may be an asteroid that was perturbed by Jupiter into an orbit that allowed it to be captured by Mars, although this hypothesis is disputed. Deimos is composed of rock rich in carbonaceous material, similar to C-type asteroids and carbonaceous chondrite meteorites.

The surface of Deimos is cratered, but appears much smoother than that of Phobos. The escape velocity from Deimos is only about 5.6 m/s, a speed that could theoretically be reached by a human performing a vertical jump.

content.name: Asteroid Belt
description: The asteroid belt is a ring-shaped region in the Solar System, centered on the Sun and spanning roughly the space between the orbits of the planets Jupiter and Mars. It consists of a large number of solid, irregularly shaped bodies called asteroids.

The asteroid belt is estimated to contain between 1.1 and 1.9 million objects larger than 1 kilometer in diameter, and millions of smaller ones. Despite their apparent abundance, asteroids are thinly distributed. On average, they are about one million kilometers apart.
About 60% of the belt's mass is contained in the four largest asteroids: Ceres, Vesta, Pallas, and Hygiea. Ceres is about 950 km in diameter, large enough to be considered a dwarf planet.

There are 3 main types of asteroids by composition. Carbon-rich (C-type) asteroids dominate the belt's outer regions and comprise over 75% of the visible asteroids. Silicate-rich (S-type) asteroids are more common toward the inner region of the belt. Metal-rich (M-type) asteroids are typically found in the middle.

notableObjects.name: Ceres
description: Ceres is a dwarf planet in the main asteroid belt between the orbits of Mars and Jupiter, the only one within the orbit of Neptune. It was previously classified as an asteroid. Ceres makes up 40% of the estimated mass of the asteroid belt.

Ceres is too small to be seen with the naked eye. It was discovered with a telescope in 1801, becoming the first known object in the asteroid belt. Little was known about Ceres until NASA's robotic spacecraft Dawn approached it in 2015.

Ceres lacks plate tectonics. The vast majority of its surface features formed either due to impacts or cryovolcanic activity. Ceres has one prominent mountain, Ahuna Mons, which appears to be a cryovolcano.

notableObjects.name: Vesta
description: Vesta is the second largest object in the asteroid belt, both by mass and by volume. It constitutes an estimated 9% of the mass of the asteroid belt.
Vesta is the brightest asteroid visible from Earth. It was discovered in 1807. NASA's robotic spacecraft Dawn orbited Vesta between July 2011 and September 2012, fully mapping its surface.

Vesta is the only known remaining rocky protoplanet of the kind that formed the terrestrial planets. Vesta is thought to consist of a metallic iron-nickel core 214-226 km in diameter, an overlying rocky mantle, and a surface crust.
The most prominent surface features of Vesta are two enormous craters: the Rheasilvia crater, centered near the south pole, and the Veneneia crater in the southern hemisphere.

notableObjects.name: Pallas
description: Pallas is the third largest asteroid in the Solar System by volume and mass. It was discovered in 1802. Pallas constitutes an estimated 7% of the mass of the asteroid belt.
The orbit of Pallas has an unusually high inclination of 34.8° to the plane of the asteroid belt, making it more difficult to reach with a spacecraft. Pallas also has a very high axial tilt of 84°.

Pallas is estimated to have a composition similar to other carbon-rich asteroids in the outer regions of the asteroid belt. It may also contain a significant amount of silicate minerals, such as olivine and pyroxene, and some proportion of metals like nickel and iron.

notableObjects.name: Hygiea
description: Hygiea is the fourth largest asteroid in the Solar System, it constitutes an estimated 3% of the mass of the asteroid belt.

Despite its size, Hygiea appears very dim when observed from Earth due to its position in the outer main belt and relatively dark surface. Hygiea was discovered in 1849, when a number of smaller asteroids was already known.
Hygiea has a nearly spherical shape. It consists primarily of rocky materials, metals, and water ice. The average surface temperature on Hygiea is about 164 K (-109 °C).

content.name: Jupiter
description: Jupiter is the largest planet in the Solar System. Its mass is greater than the mass of all the other planets combined.

Jupiter is a gas giant. It is primarily composed of hydrogen (about 75%) and helium (about 24%), with trace amounts of other elements. Unlike terrestrial planets, it does not have a well-defined solid surface.

Jupiter's atmosphere is characterized by alternating bands of light and dark clouds running parallel to its equator, caused by strong winds reaching the speeds of 600 kilometers per hour. The most prominent feature in Jupiter's atmosphere is the Great Red Spot, a massive storm system larger than Earth that has been observed for centuries.

Jupiter has dozens of natural satellites. The four largest moons, known as the Galilean moons after their discoverer Galileo Galilei, are Io, Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto. These moons are some of the most geologically active bodies in the solar system, with features such as volcanoes, ice-covered oceans, and rocky surfaces.

Jupiter has been visited by several spacecraft, including the Pioneer and Voyager missions in the 1970s and 1980s, the Galileo orbiter in the 1990s, and the Juno spacecraft, which arrived in orbit around Jupiter in 2016.

moons.name: Ganymede
description: Ganymede is Jupiter's largest satellite and the largest moon in the Solar System. It is also the largest Solar System object without a substantial atmosphere.

Ganymede is composed of silicate rock and water in approximately equal proportions. It is the only moon in the Solar System with a substantial magnetic field, possibly generated by convection in its liquid iron core. However, Ganymede's magnetic field is buried within the much larger magnetic field of Jupiter.

Ganymede's surface consists of two main types of terrain: lighter regions, generally crossed by extensive grooves and ridges, which cover about two-thirds of Ganymede; and darker regions saturated with impact craters.

Ganymede was discovered in 1610, along with the other Galilean moons. Several spacecraft have performed close flybys of Ganymede: two Pioneer and two Voyager spacecraft made a single flyby each between 1973 and 1979, the Galileo spacecraft made six passes between 1996 and 2000, and the Juno spacecraft performed two flybys in 2019 and 2021.

moons.name: Callisto
description: Callisto is the second largest moon of Jupiter the third largest moon in the Solar System. It is the outermost of the four large Galilean moons.
Callisto is composed of approximately equal amounts of rock and ice. It has the lowest density and surface gravity of Jupiter's major moons.

Callisto's surface is the oldest and most heavily cratered in the Solar System. It does not show any signatures of subsurface processes such as plate tectonics or volcanism, with no signs that geological activity in general has ever occurred.

Callisto was discovered in 1610, along with the other Galilean moons. The Pioneer 10 and Pioneer 11 spacecraft approached Callisto in the early 1970s. In 1979, Voyager 1 and Voyager 2 took high-resolution images of more than half of Callisto's surface and made precise measurements of its temperature, mass, and shape. The Galileo spacecraft had eight close encounters with Callisto between 1994 and 2003.

moons.name: Io
description: Io is the innermost and second smallest of Jupiter's four Galilean moons. It has the highest density of any moon and the strongest surface gravity of any moon.

Io is composed primarily of silicate rock surrounding a molten iron core. Most of Io's surface is covered by extensive plains with a frosty coating of sulfur and sulfur dioxide. It is also dotted with mountains uplifted by extensive compression at the base of Io's silicate crust.

Io is the most geologically active object in the Solar System, as the result of tidal heating from friction generated in Io's interior as it is pulled between Jupiter and the other Galilean moons. Volcanoes produce plumes of sulfur and sulfur dioxide that rise as high as 500 km above the surface.

Io was discovered in 1610, along with the other Galilean moons. In 1979, the two Voyager spacecraft revealed Io to be a geologically active world. The Galileo spacecraft made several close flybys in the 1990s and early 2000s. Further observations have been made by Cassini-Huygens in 2000, New Horizons in 2007, and Juno in 2017.

moons.name: Europa
description: Europa is the smallest of the four Galilean moons orbiting Jupiter.

Europa is composed of silicate rock. It has a water-ice crust and probably an iron-nickel core. Europa has the smoothest surface of any known solid body in the Solar System. The white-beige surface is geologically young and has very few impact craters, only crossed by faint brown cracks and streaks. Europa has a very thin atmosphere, consisting primarily of oxygen.

An ocean of liquid water may exist beneath Europa's icy surface and could potentially harbor extraterrestrial life. The prevailing model suggests that heat from tidal flexing keeps the ocean liquid and drives ice movement similar to plate tectonics, absorbing chemicals from the surface into the ocean below.

Europa was discovered in 1610, along with the other Galilean moons. Modern exploration began with the Pioneer 10 and Pioneer 11 spacecraft in 1973 and 1974, followed by the two Voyager probes in 1979. The Galileo space probe orbited Jupiter from 1995 to 2003, providing the most detailed examination of Europa to date.

content.name: Saturn
description: Saturn is the sixth planet from the Sun and the second-largest in the Solar System.

Saturn is a gas giant. It does not have a well-defined solid surface. It is primarily composed of hydrogen (about 96%) and helium (about 3%) with trace amounts of other elements. Saturn has a lower density than water.
Saturn has banded cloud patterns running parallel to its equator, caused by strong winds in its upper atmosphere. It also features a hexagonally shaped jet stream near the planet's north pole.

Saturn is famous for its large ring system, which consists of icy particles and bodies ranging in size from micrometers to several meters. The rings extend tens of thousands of kilometers from the planet's equator, but are only about 10 meters thick.

Saturn has over a hundred natural satellites. Notable moons of Saturn include Titan, the second-largest moon in the Solar System, and Enceladus, which has geysers of water vapor erupting from its surface.

Saturn has been visited by several probes, including the Pioneer and Voyager missions in the 1970s and 1980s, which provided the first close-up images of the planet. The Cassini-Huygens mission orbited Saturn from 2004 to 2017, providing detailed observations of the planet, its rings, and its moons.

moons.name: Titan
description: Titan is the largest moon of Saturn and the second-largest moon in the Solar System.

Titan is primarily composed of ice and rocky material. The surface is generally smooth and geologically young, with few impact craters, although mountains and several possible cryovolcanoes have been found. Titan has permanent lakes and seas of liquid hydrocarbons, which makes it the only known object in the Solar System besides Earth with clear evidence of stable bodies of surface liquid.

The dense atmosphere of Titan is mainly composed of nitrogen and methane, with hydrocarbon clouds and heavy haze of nitrogen compounds. Titan's methane cycle nearly resembles Earth's water cycle, creating seasonal weather patterns. However, Titan has a much lower average surface temperature of about -179 °C.

Titan is never visible to the naked eye, it was discovered by Christiaan Huygens via telescope observations in 1655. Titan was photographed by Pioneer and Voyager probes in 1979-1981. More detailed exploration was made by the Cassini probe in 2004-2010. The Huygens probe landed on Titan in 2005, making direct measurements of atmospheric and surface conditions.

moons.name: Rhea
description: Rhea is the second-largest natural satellite of Saturn. It is the smallest body in the Solar System that is confirmed to be in hydrostatic equilibrium. Rhea has a nearly circular orbit with an inclination of less than a degree from Saturn's equatorial plane. Rhea is tidally locked, which means the same hemisphere always faces towards Saturn.

Rhea has a fairly low density, composed of roughly three-quarters ice and only one-quarter rock. The surface of Rhea is heavily cratered, with distinct leading and trailing hemispheres. It has highly reflective ice cliffs that appear as bright shimmering streaks visible from space. The surface temperature varies between -174 °C and -220 °C.
Rhea was discovered in 1672 by Giovanni Cassini. It has been visited by both Voyager probes in the early 1980s and was the subject of close targeted flybys by the Cassini orbiter in 2005-2013.

moons.name: Iapetus
description: Iapetus is the third-largest moon of Saturn and the outermost of Saturn's large moons. It also has the most inclined orbital plane among the regular satellites, the inclination varies between 5° and 21° over the period of about 3400 years.

The low density of Iapetus indicates that it is mostly composed of ice, with only about 20% rocky materials. The leading hemisphere and sides are dark, with a slight reddish-brown coloring, while most of the trailing hemisphere and poles are bright.

Iapetus is heavily cratered, with large impact basins, at least five of which are over 350 km wide. Iapetus has a unique equatorial ridge about 1300 km long, 20 km wide, and up to 20 km high, making it some of the tallest mountains in the Solar System.
Iapetus was discovered by Giovanni Cassini in 1671. It was photographed by Voyager probes flying by in 1980-1981 and further explored by the Cassini spacecraft in 2004-2007.

moons.name: Dione
description: Dione is the fourth-largest moon of Saturn. Dione is currently in a 1:2 orbital resonance with Enceladus, completing one orbit of Saturn for every two orbits Enceladus. It also has two small co-orbital moons, Helene and Polydeuces, located 60 degrees ahead of and behind Dione respectively.

Dione is composed of an icy mantle and crust overlying a silicate rocky core, with rock and water ice roughly equal in mass. Some models suggest that the lowermost part of the ice layer could be in the form of an internal liquid saltwater ocean.
Dione's leading hemisphere is heavily cratered and is uniformly bright. Its trailing hemisphere contains a network of bright ice cliffs.

Dione was discovered by Giovanni Cassini in 1684. It was first imaged up-close by the Voyager 1 space probe in 1980. Later, the Cassini spacecraft made multiple flybys of Dione throughout the 2000s and 2010s.

moons.name: Tethys
description: Tethys is the fifth-largest moon of Saturn. Tethys has two small co-orbital moons, Telesto and Calypso, orbiting 60° ahead and 60° behind respectively.

Tethys is composed almost entirely of water ice. It is unknown whether Tethys has a rocky core, but the radius of such a core would not exceed 145 km. The existence of a subsurface ocean is considered unlikely.

The surface of Tethys is one of the most reflective in the Solar System. It mostly consists of hilly terrain dominated by craters more than 40 km in diameter. The leading hemisphere of Tethys is slightly brighter than the trailing one.

Tethys was discovered by Giovanni Cassini in 1684. It has been approached and observed by several space probes, including Pioneer 11 in 1979, Voyager 1 in 1980, Voyager 2 in 1981, and the Cassini spacecraft making the most extensive observations between 2004 and 2017.

moons.name: Enceladus
description: Enceladus is the sixth-largest moon of Saturn. It is currently in a 2:1 orbital resonance with Dione, completing two orbits around Saturn for every orbit of Dione. This resonance results in tidal deformation of Enceladus, with the dissipated heat serving as the main source for its geologic activity.

More than 100 cryovolcanoes near the south pole of Enceladus shoot geyser-like jets of water vapour, molecular hydrogen, other volatiles, and solid material into space, with some of it falling back as snow. Enceladus is covered by clean, freshly deposited snow hundreds of meters thick, making it one of the most reflective bodies in the Solar System. Its surface temperature reaches only -198 °C, far colder than a light-absorbing body would be.

Enceladus likely has a large subsurface ocean of liquid water with a thickness of around 10 km. Chemical analysis of Enceladus's plumes suggests hydrothermal activity possibly driving complex chemistry. This, in turn, leads to the possibility of extraterrestrial life in the subsurface ocean.

Enceladus was discovered by William Herschel in 1789. The Voyager probes captured the first close-up images of Enceladus in 1980 and 1981. The Cassini spacecraft made multiple targeted flybys between 2004 and 2015, gathering significant information about the surface of Enceladus and its chemical composition.

moons.name: Mimas
description: Mimas is the seventh-largest natural satellite of Saturn. With a mean diameter of only 396 kilometers, it is the smallest astronomical body known to be roughly spherical due to its own gravity.

Mimas is composed mostly of water ice with only a small amount of rock. It may have a liquid ocean beneath its icy surface. Mimas is heavily cratered and shows little signs of recent geological activity.

A notable surface feature is Herschel, one of the largest craters relative to the size of the parent body in the Solar System. Herschel measures 139 kilometers across, about one-third of the mean diameter of Mimas.

Mimas was discovered by William Herschel in 1789. It was approached by several space probes: Pioneer 11 flew by in 1979, Voyager 1 in 1980, Voyager 2 in 1981. The Cassini spacecraft made its closest flyby of Mimas in 2010.

content.name: Uranus
description: Uranus is the seventh planet from the Sun. It is a gaseous cyan-colored ice giant.
The planet consists mostly of water, ammonia, and methane in a supercritical phase of matter.  Uranus has the lowest minimum temperature (-224 °C) out of all the Solar System's planets.
Uranus' atmosphere is predominantly composed of hydrogen and helium. It has a complex layered cloud structure and relatively few visible cloud features or turbulence.

Uranus is tilted at an angle of about 82 degrees relative to its orbit. This means that the planet essentially rotates on its side as it orbits the Sun. Each pole experiences about 42 years of continuous sunlight followed by 42 years of darkness.

Uranus has over 20 known moons. It also has a system of narrow, dark rings composed of dust particles and small chunks of ice. Unlike the more prominent rings of Saturn, Uranus' rings are relatively faint and were not discovered until 1977.
Uranus has only been visited by one spacecraft, Voyager 2 in 1986, which provided the first and only close-up images of the planet and its moons. No other spacecraft has flown by Uranus since then.

moons.name: Titania
description: Titania is the largest moon of Uranus and the eighth-largest moon in the Solar System. Titania's orbit has a small eccentricity and is inclined very little relative to the equator of Uranus. Titania is a tidally locked satellite, with one side always pointing towards Uranus.

Titania consists of approximately equal amounts of ice and rock, and probably has a rocky core and an icy mantle. Its surface is relatively dark and slightly red in color, covered with numerous impact craters reaching up to 326 km in diameter. The surface is also cut by a system of enormous canyons formed when its interior expanded during the later stages of its evolution.

Titania was discovered by William Herschel in 1787. The only close-up images of Titania were captured by the Voyager 2 probe during its flyby in 1986. No other spacecraft has ever visited it since then.

moons.name: Oberon
description: Oberon is the outermost and second-largest major moon of Uranus. Its orbit has a small eccentricity and a low inclination relative to the equator of Uranus. Oberon is tidally locked, with one side always pointing towards Uranus.

Similarly to Titania, Oberon  consists of approximately equal amounts of ice and rock and probably has a rocky core and an icy mantle. The surface of Oberon, which is dark and slightly red in color, appears to have been primarily shaped by asteroid and comet impacts. It is covered by numerous impact craters reaching 210 km in diameter.

Oberon was discovered by William Herschel in 1787. The only close-up images of Oberon were captured by the Voyager 2 probe during its flyby in 1986. No other spacecraft has ever visited it since then.

moons.name: Umbriel
description: Umbriel is the third-largest moon of Uranus. Umbriel is tidally locked, with one side always pointing towards Uranus.

Umbriel consists mainly of ice with a substantial fraction of rock. It likely has a rocky core and an icy mantle. The surface of Umbriel  is the darkest among Uranian moons, covered by numerous impact craters reaching 210 km in diameter. The most prominent surface feature is a ring of bright material (likely, carbon dioxide ice) on the floor of Wunda crater.

Umbriel was discovered by William Lassell in 1851. The only close-up images of Umbriel were captured by the Voyager 2 probe during its flyby in 1986. No other spacecraft has ever visited it since then.

moons.name: Ariel
description: Ariel is the fourth-largest moon of Uranus. It orbits and rotates in the planet's equatorial plane, which is nearly perpendicular to its orbit. This gives Ariel an extreme seasonal cycle.

Ariel is composed of ice and rocky material in roughly equal parts.  Its surface can be divided into three terrain types: cratered terrain (oldest and most extensive, centered on the south pole), ridged terrain (bands of ridges and troughs hundreds of kilometers in extent), and plains (smooth areas, likely formed through volcanic processes). Ariel is the most reflective of Uranian moons.

Ariel was discovered by William Lassell in 1851. The only close-up images of Ariel were captured by the Voyager 2 probe during its flyby in 1986. The Hubble Space Telescope captured a rare transit made by Ariel on Uranus in 2006.

moons.name: Miranda
description: Miranda is the smallest and innermost of the five round satellites of Uranus. Of all round moons of the Solar System, it has the smallest orbit around a major planet, with an orbital period of only 34 hours.

Miranda is the least dense of Uranian round satellites, which suggests a composition of more than 60% water ice. The surface of Miranda is a mosaic of highly varied zones, with fractures, faults, valleys, craters, ridges, gorges, depressions, cliffs, and terraces.

Miranda is almost invisible to amateur telescopes. It was discovered by Gerard Kuiper in 1948. The only close-up images of Miranda were captured by the Voyager 2 probe during its flyby in 1986. No other spacecraft has ever visited it since then.

content.name: Neptune
description: Neptune is the eighth and farthest known planet from the Sun.
Neptune is an ice giant. It no well-defined solid surface. Neptune's composition includes hydrogen, helium, water, ammonia, and methane.

Neptune's atmosphere is known for its dynamic and turbulent weather patterns, including winds that can reach speeds of 2,000 kilometers per hour. Because of its great distance from the Sun, Neptune's outer atmosphere is one of the coldest places in the Solar System, with temperatures at its cloud tops approaching -218 °C.
Neptune has over a dozen of known moons, with the largest and most notable being Triton. The planet also has a faint system of rings composed primarily of dust particles and small rocks.

Neptune is not visible to the naked eye and is the only planet in the Solar System that was found based on mathematical predictions. Neptune has only been visited by one spacecraft, Voyager 2, which flew by the planet in 1989 and provided the first and only close-up images of the planet and its moons.

moons.name: Triton
description: Triton is Neptune's largest natural satellite. It is the only moon of Neptune massive enough to be rounded under its own gravity. Triton is the only large moon in the Solar System with a retrograde orbit, revolving in the direction opposite to Neptune's rotation.
Triton is larger than all known dwarf planets. It may have originated in the Kuiper belt as a dwarf planet, captured into orbit by Neptune's gravity.

Triton is composed of 30 to 45 percent water ice by mass, with the remainder consisting mostly of rock and metal. It has an icy crust atop a probable subsurface ocean of liquid water and a solid, rocky-metallic core at its center.

Triton is one of the most geologically active bodies in the Solar System. Its surface is less than 100 million years old on average, covered with frozen nitrogen, and has very few impact craters. Triton has an atmosphere that consists mostly of nitrogen, with trace amounts of carbon monoxide and methane. Its average surface temperature is around -237 °C.

Triton was discovered by William Lassell in 1846, just 17 days after the discovery of Neptune. The only close-up images of Triton were captured by the Voyager 2 probe during its flyby in 1989. No other spacecraft has ever visited it since then.

content.name: Kuiper Belt
description: The Kuiper belt is a ring-shaped region in the Solar System, centered on the Sun and extending from the orbit of Neptune at 30 AU to approximately 50 AU from the Sun. It is similar to the asteroid belt (but about 20 times larger) and consists mainly of small, irregularly shaped bodies.

The Kuiper belt is home to most of the objects that astronomers generally accept as dwarf planets: Pluto, Haumea, Makemake, Quaoar, and Orcus. Pluto was considered a planet prior to 2006, but its status was changed following the discovery of other similar bodies.

Most Kuiper belt objects are composed largely of a mixture of rock and frozen methane, ammonia, and water. The temperature of the belt is only about 50 K (-223 °C), so many compounds that would be gaseous closer to the Sun remain solid.
The first spacecraft to explore the Kuiper belt was New Horizons, which flew by Pluto in 2015 and by Arrokoth in 2019. The exploration continues, and new missions are planned.

notableObjects.name: Pluto
description: Pluto is a dwarf planet in the Kuiper belt. It is the ninth largest and tenth most massive known object to directly orbit the Sun. It is also the largest known trans-Neptunian object by volume. Pluto's eccentric orbit periodically brings it closer to the Sun than Neptune, but a stable 2:3 orbital resonance prevents them from colliding.

Pluto is the first known object in the Kuiper belt, discovered in 1930. It was classified as the 9th planet until its status was changed in 2006 following the discovery of other similar objects in the Kuiper belt. In 2015, the New Horizons spacecraft made a close approach to Pluto, sending back a large amount of images and other scientific data.

Pluto has five known moons: Charon (with a diameter just over half that of Pluto), Styx, Nix, Kerberos, and Hydra. Pluto and Charon are sometimes considered a binary system because the barycenter of their orbits does not lie within either body, and they are tidally locked.

Pluto's surface is quite varied, with large differences in both brightness and color. The plains on Pluto's surface are composed of more than 98% nitrogen ice, with traces of methane and carbon monoxide. The mountains are made of water ice.

notableObjects.name: Haumea
description: Haumea is a dwarf planet, discovered in 2004. It is estimated to be the third largest known trans-Neptunian object. It has two small natural satellites, Hiiaka and Namaka.

Although Haumea's shape has not been directly observed, calculations suggest that it is close to an ellipsoid, with its major axis twice as long as its minor axis. Haumea rapidly rotates with a period of just under 4 hours, which causes it to be elongated.

Haumea is as bright as snow. Modeling of the surface spectra suggested that 66% to 80% of the Haumean surface appears to be pure crystalline water ice. Inorganic cyanide salts, such as copper potassium cyanide, may also be present.
In 2017, astronomers announced the discovery of a ring system around Haumea, representing the first ring system discovered for a trans-Neptunian object and a dwarf planet.

notableObjects.name: Makemake
description: Makemake is a dwarf planet, discovered in 2005. It has one known natural satellite, about 175 km in diameter.
The rotation period of Makemake is estimated at 22.83 hours, which is relatively long for a dwarf planet. This may be explained by tidal acceleration from its satellite.

Makemake lacks a substantial atmosphere and has extremely low average temperature, about 40 K (-230 °C). Its surface is covered with methane, ethane, and possibly nitrogen ices. Makemake shows signs of geothermal activity and thus may be capable of supporting active geology and harboring an active subsurface ocean.

notableObjects.name: Quaoar
description: Quaoar is a dwarf planet, discovered in 2002. It has a shape of an elongated ellipsoid with the size of about 1286×1080×932 km.
Quaoar has one known moon, Weywot, which is about 200 km in diameter and was discovered in 2007. In 2023, astronomers announced the discovery of two thin rings orbiting Quaoar.

Quaoar has a dark, moderately red surface that reflects about 12% of the visible light it receives from the Sun. Its mean temperature is about 44 K (-229 °C). Spectroscopic observations revealed signs of crystalline water ice and ammonia hydrate on the surface, with small quantities of solid methane and ethane.

notableObjects.name: Orcus
description: Orcus is a dwarf planet, discovered in 2004. It has one large moon, Vanth, with a diameter of 443 km, about half the size of Orcus.

Much like Pluto, Orcus is locked in a 2:3 orbital resonance with Neptune, making two revolutions around the Sun to every three of Neptune's. Orcus is at aphelion near the time when Pluto is at perihelion and vice versa. The perihelion of Orcus's orbit is around 120° from that of Pluto, while the eccentricities and inclinations are similar.

The surface of Orcus is relatively bright, reflecting about 23% of light, and neutral in color. It is rich in water ice, predominantly in crystalline form, which may be related to past cryovolcanic activity. Other compounds, like methane or ammonia, may also be present on it.

content.name: Scattered Disc
description: The scattered disc a torus-shaped region in the Solar System, centered on the Sun. It is sparsely populated by small icy bodies of irregular shape, which are among the coldest and most distant objects in the Solar System. They are composed largely of frozen water and methane.

The scattered disc objects have highly eccentric orbits that approach the Sun at about 30-35 AU and can extend well beyond 100 AU, sometimes with inclinations as high as 40°. In contrast to the Kuiper belt, the scattered disc objects can be disturbed by Neptune's gravity at their closest approaches to the Sun.

Because of its unstable nature, astronomers now consider the scattered disc to be the place of origin for most short-period comets, as those may have been drawn into the inner Solar System by the gravity of Neptune and other outer planets.

notableObjects.name: Eris
description: Eris is the most massive and second largest known dwarf planet in the Solar System. It was discovered in 2005 and remains the largest known object in the Solar System that has not been visited by a spacecraft. Eris has one large known moon, Dysnomia, with an estimated diameter of 615 km.

The surface of Eris appears almost white and uniform. Its temperature is estimated to vary from about 30 K to 56 K (-243 °C to -217 °C). Infrared spectroscopy revealed the presence of methane ice and substantial quantities of nitrogen ice, indicating that the surface may be similar to that of Pluto, but condensation of methane spread uniformly over the surface possibly reduces any contrasts.

notableObjects.name: Gonggong
description: Gonggong is a dwarf planet, discovered in 2007. It has a highly eccentric and inclined orbit in a 3:10 orbital resonance with Neptune, which means it completes three orbits around the Sun for every ten orbits completed by Neptune. Gonggong has one natural satellite, Xiangliu, about 100 km in diameter.

The surface of Gonggong reflects about 14% of light. Gonggong is among the reddest known trans-Neptunian objects. Spectroscopic observations revealed significant amounts of ethane ice and the presence of methane and carbon dioxide. It is also likely to contain trace amounts of other volatiles, including ammonia, carbon monoxide, and possibly nitrogen.
